Paper_Metadata.Paper_Title: Nanotwinned precipitates induced ultra-strong AlCoCrFeNi2.1 eutectic high-entropy alloy through additive manufacturing

items.Note: Nominal composition derived from alloy designation AlCoCrFeNi2.1 with Ni content 2.1 times equiatomic; measured composition from as-built sample.

Process_Text.original: The PBF-LB process was conducted using an EOS M100 Laser-sintering system (Germany) equipped with a 200W fiber laser and a laser spot diameter of 40μm. The adopted optimized parameters were a laser power (P) of 100W, a scan speed (v) of 1000mm/s, a hatch spacing (h) of 0.05mm and a layer thickness (t) of 0.02mm. Prior to the AM process, a 20μm thick layer of powder was deposited onto a 304 stainless-steel build platform without preheating in a high-purity argon environment (<1000ppm oxygen). A laser beam traversed the powder feedstock along a predefined path, generating sufficient heat to melt the powder and form a melt pool, which subsequently solidified into a fused layer. After each scan, a new layer of powder was deposited, and the laser scanning direction was rotated by 67° relative to the previous layer. This operation was repeated for each subsequent layer. The final printed rectangular plates, with dimensions of 40mm (length) × 8mm (width) × 4mm (building height), exhibited a relative density exceeding 99%.
Process_Text.simplified: Laser powder bed fusion using EOS M100 with 100W laser power, 1000mm/s scan speed, 50μm hatch spacing, 20μm layer thickness, 40μm spot diameter, argon atmosphere, no preheating, 67° rotation between layers.

Process_Info.Equipment: EOS M100 Laser-sintering system (200W fiber laser)

Microstructure_Text.original: A dual-phase hierarchical microstructure is achieved in the as-built AlCoCrFeNi2.1 EHEA. The 3D reconstructed SEM image displays regular and smooth scan tracks, measuring approximately 52μm, which corresponds to the preset hatch space of 50μm. The solidified melt pools are partially overlapped with their adjacent ones, each featuring a mixture of nano-lamellar and cellular eutectic colonies. The as-built EHEA exhibits a lower volume fraction of the FCC phase (16.4%) and a much higher volume fraction of the B2 phase (83.6%). The lamellar colonies are composed of alternating FCC and ordered B2 nanolayers with average thicknesses of λ_FCC = 87 nm and λ_B2 = 160 nm. Cellular colonies are primarily composed of the BCC phase with a high density of dislocations, adopting an ordered-B2 structure encapsulated by a disordered FCC interfacial nanolayer (~14 nm). The FCC and B2 phases in the cellular colony exhibit the Nishiyama-Wasserman (N-W) relationship. Elemental analysis shows that the B2 phase is Al-enriched compared to the FCC phase. Nanoscale-modulated chemical inhomogeneity is observed within the B2 colonies, suggesting spinodal decomposition.
Microstructure_Text.simplified: Hierarchical dual-phase microstructure consisting of 16.4% FCC and 83.6% B2. Contains lamellar colonies (FCC lamellae ~87 nm, B2 lamellae ~160 nm) and cellular colonies (B2 cells ~800 nm with ~14 nm FCC shell). High dislocation density. Compositional inhomogeneity within B2 phase.

Properties_Info.Test_Condition: Room temperature tensile, perpendicular to build direction

Composition_Info.Note: Same nominal composition as as-built, annealed condition does not change bulk composition.

Process_Text.original: The as-built samples were subjected to 600°C for 8h to trigger nanoprecipitation. Post-heat treatments were performed in a muffle furnace at 600°C for various durations, followed by water quenching.
Process_Text.simplified: Annealing at 600°C for 8 h followed by water quenching.

Microstructure_Text.original: The annealed sample retains a mixture of nano-lamellar and cellular eutectic colonies, with only slight coarsening observed. Specifically, λ_FCC and λ_B2 increase from 87 to 96 nm and from 160 to 176 nm, respectively, while the average diameter of the cellular structure grows from ~549 to ~653 nm. At the micro-scale, nanoscale precipitates are observed in both colonies after annealing. In the FCC phase, ordered L1₂ precipitates are identified. Whereas, high-density needle-like nanoprecipitates are evenly distributed within the B2 phases. These nanoprecipitates average ~180 nm in length and ~20 nm in diameter, with volume fractions of 20% in lamellar colonies and 45% in cellular colonies. High-magnification BF-TEM images reveal extremely fine lamellae substructures within the needle-like precipitates, distinguishable by numerous parallel lamellae. Atomic-resolution HAADF-STEM imaging confirms that these lamellae correspond to nanotubes. Two types of precipitates exist: thicker type I precipitates have an FCC structure containing ultrafine nanotubes with twin thickness ranging from 0.4 nm to 16 nm (avg. 2.4 nm); thinner type II precipitates feature an HCP structure interspersed with high-density stacking faults. About 76% of needle-like nanoprecipitates are FCC-type. Compositional analysis shows that the NT FCC nanoprecipitates consist of Co, Cr, Fe, and Ni in nearly equiatomic ratios (approximately 24.4 at.% each) and ~2.4 at.% Al, corresponding to the CoCrFeNiAl₀.₁ HEA.
Microstructure_Text.simplified: Slightly coarsened lamellar (FCC ~96 nm, B2 ~176 nm) and cellular structures (~653 nm). High-density needle-like precipitates in B2 matrix: ~180 nm length, ~20 nm diameter, volume fraction 20-45%. Predominantly FCC-structured precipitates contain nanotwins (avg. twin thickness 2.4 nm). Minor HCP-structured precipitates with stacking faults. L1₂ precipitates in FCC phase.
